question.text: What is 'moneyness' of an option and how is it classified?

answer.text: ITM (In-The-Money): option has positive intrinsic value. ATM (At-The-Money): strike equals spot. OTM (Out-Of-The-Money): option has zero intrinsic value and would not be exercised at current prices
Moneyness refers to how much money the option costs (premium)
ITM means the option is profitable; OTM means it has expired worthless
Moneyness only applies to American-style options, not European-style

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Call: ITM when S > K, ATM when S ≈ K, OTM when S < K. Put: ITM when K > S, ATM when K ≈ S, OTM when K < S. Moneyness determines intrinsic value and the likelihood of exercise. Deep ITM options behave more like the underlying. **The Trap:** Moneyness doesn't mean profitability — an ITM option can still produce a net loss after accounting for the premium paid. Moneyness applies to all option styles. **Cross-Exam Context:** Moneyness classification is identical across equity (VIII), currency (I), IR (IV), and commodity (XVI) options. Universal concept for Series XIII. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** ATM options have the highest time value and are most sensitive to volatility changes — they're the preferred strike for traders expressing volatility views.
